div.div: Nothing special — just the game board on page 1.

This entry contains three quick alphabet games you can play with no equipment:
ALLITERATION CHALLENGE: The first player picks a letter. Everyone takes turns saying a complete sentence where every word starts with that letter. For example, with "B": "Big bears bake brilliant biscuits behind barns." If you hesitate too long or repeat a word, you're out. Last player standing wins, then picks a new letter.
ALPHABET CATEGORIES: Choose a category (animals, cities, foods, etc.). Players go around the circle naming one item for each letter in order — A, B, C, and so on. Skip difficult letters like X and Z if you like. If a player can't think of one before the group counts to ten, they're out.
ANAGRAM NAMES: Each player writes their full name on a slip of paper and passes it to the left. You have two minutes to rearrange the letters of the name you received into as many real words as possible. The player who finds the most words wins. Proper nouns don't count.
Alphabet games were staple Victorian schoolroom entertainment that doubled as genuinely educational — building vocabulary while having fun.
Players use letters to form words, sentences, or transpose names in clever alphabetical challenges.

svg.text: Source: Cassell's Book of In-door Amusements, Card Games, and Fireside Fun (1881) · Heritage Parlor
